plainMeaning: Picturing the holy community as Mount Meru, this verse praises its lightning-bright summit crowned by the noblest monks, and its grove crowded with wish-granting trees of every virtue, heavy with fruit and flowers.
recitation: viṇāyaṇāya pavara muṇivara, kuraṃta vijju-jjalaṃta siharassa | viviha guṇa kappa-rukkhaga, phala-bhara kusumāula vaṇassa ||

wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. vinaya ('humility, right discipline, the leading-away of fault') > Prakrit viṇaya; here qualifying the munis on the peak.
wordByWord.notes: OCR/lexical reading uncertain; could also be parsed as a descriptor of the summit. Read with vinaya in line with the hymn's repeated stress on monastic discipline.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pravara ('chief, best') > Prakrit pavara, by loss of intervocalic -r- cluster and softening.
Skt. muni ('sage, silent ascetic') + vara ('best') = muni-vara; Prakrit muṇivara. A tatpuruṣa 'excellent among munis'.
Present participle of √kṛ ('to do, make'); Skt. kurvat > Prakrit kuraṃta. Here: the munis 'making/gracing' the peak resplendent.
wordByWord.notes: Participial reading; an alternative would take it as a flower-name (kuraṇṭa), but the grove-flowers belong to line 2, so the participle of √kṛ is preferred.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vidyut ('lightning') > Prakrit vijju + jvalat/jvalanta ('blazing', pres. part. of √jval) > jjalaṃta, with assimilation. A simile-compound 'lightning-blazing'.

wordByWord.gloss: of the peak, of the summit
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śikhara ('peak, crest') > Prakrit sihara; genitive singular -assa (Skt. -asya). The -ssa ties this clause to the implied Meru/Saṅgha of the following verse.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive depending on 'saṅgha-mahāmandara giri', resolved by 'vandāmi' in v.17.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vividha ('of many kinds') > Prakrit viviha, by softening of the medial -dh-.
Skt. guṇa ('quality, merit, strand') > Prakrit guṇa; here the spiritual virtues of the Saṅgha, embodied as wish-trees.
Skt. kalpa ('wish, the granting of a wish') + vṛkṣa ('tree') = kalpa-vṛkṣa > Prakrit kappa-rukkha, with diminutive/collective -ga; conjuncts assimilated (lp>pp, kṣ>kkh).

wordByWord.gloss: laden with fruit; a burden of fruit
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. phala ('fruit, result') + bhara ('load, weight') = phala-bhara; Prakrit unchanged. Bahuvrīhi: '[the grove] whose load is its fruit'.

wordByWord.gloss: thronged with flowers, dense with blossoms
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. kusuma ('flower') + ākula ('crowded, filled') = kusumākula > Prakrit kusumāula, with vocalization of -k- to glide and hiatus.

wordByWord.gloss: of the grove, of the forest
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vana ('forest, grove') > Prakrit vaṇa (n>ṇ); genitive singular -assa. Evokes Meru's celestial Nandana grove.
wordByWord.notes: Parallel genitive to siharassa; the two halves give the peak and the grove of the one Saṅgha-Meru.

literalTranslation: [Of that Meru] whose lightning-blazing peak the most excellent, discipline-endowed best of sages grace; whose grove [teems] with wish-fulfilling trees of manifold virtues, laden with fruit and thronged with blossoms.
idiomaticTranslation: Its summit flashes like lightning, crowned by the foremost of disciplined sages; its grove is a paradise of wish-granting trees, each one a different virtue, bowed low with fruit and ablaze with flowers — such is the great Mountain that is the Saṅgha.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā gives two more limbs of the Saṅgha-as-Meru figure, both in the genitive (siharassa, vaṇassa) that hangs unresolved until the 'vandāmi' of the next verse. The first half lifts the eye to the summit: the peak does not merely shine but 'blazes like lightning' (vijju-jjalanta), and what crowns it is not snow or gold but living holiness — 'the most excellent, discipline-endowed best of sages' (viṇāya pavara muṇivara) who abide there (kuranta). The mapping is exact: as gods and seers throng Meru's heights, so the Saṅgha's loftiest point is its company of accomplished munis, and their adornment is vinaya — the humility and self-discipline that is the root of all monastic virtue. The second half drops to the slopes: the grove (vaṇa), unmistakably the celestial Nandana of Meru, is planted with 'wish-fulfilling trees of manifold virtues' (viviha-guṇa-kappa-rukkhaga). Here the poet makes the single most pointed move of the verse — every distinct guṇa of the community is itself a kalpavṛkṣa. And these trees are not barren ornaments: they are 'laden with fruit' (phala-bhara) and 'thronged with blossoms' (kusumāula), the bending boughs and crowded flowers signalling a virtue that actually yields — flower of practice, fruit of realization. The verse thus pairs height with fertility: the munis are the unreachable summit, the virtues the generous, fruiting grove that draws and sustains all who approach.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse's theological weight sits on the kalpavṛkṣa simile. A worldly wish-tree grants sensual desires; here the trees are the Saṅgha's virtues, and what they yield is spiritual — the 'fruit' (phala) is the karmic result of right practice, the maturing of the path toward liberation. By making each guṇa a separate tree, the verse affirms that the community's holiness is not a single abstraction but a whole flowering ecology of qualities (the three jewels, the great vows, restraint, study) each bearing its own fruit. The munis on the lightning-bright peak embody the apex of that same ascent: vinaya named first because, in Jain pedagogy, humility before the guru and the teaching is the precondition of all higher attainment.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter is invited to two acts at once: to look up — honoring the disciplined munis as the living summit of the tradition — and to look around at the grove, recognizing that virtue is meant to fruit, not merely to be admired. Cultivate a guṇa as one tends a tree: rooted in vinaya, it should in time blossom (visible practice) and bear fruit (real change), offering shade and nourishment to others.

crossReferences.text: Nandī-sūtra, opening maṅgala — the Saṅgha-Meru gāthās (peaks, Nandana grove, jewel-caves)
Cosmographic descriptions of Meru's Nandana-vana and its kalpavṛkṣas
Virtue and reverence (vinaya) as the root of liberation-practice

sources.consultedFor: Identification of vaṇa with Meru's celestial Nandana grove

sources.title: Śikhara — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of sihara/śikhara as 'peak, crest, summit'

sources.title: Munivara — definitions ('excellent sage')

sources.title: Nandi Sutra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Source identity of the gāthās as the opening Saṅgha-praise of the Nandī-sūtra

notes: The two genitive endings (siharassa, vaṇassa) are not resolved within this verse: they qualify the 'saṅgha-mahāmandara giri' that is finally bowed to by 'vandāmi' in the following verse (17), so v.16 should be read as a descriptive clause carried over. Line 2 is secure (viviha guṇa kappa-rukkhaga / phala-bhara kusumāula vaṇassa). In line 1, 'viṇāyaṇāya' (read here from vinaya, 'discipline/humility') and 'kuraṃta' (read as the present participle of √kṛ, 'gracing/making') are the least certain tokens; OCR mātrā/conjunct rendering of the Anchalgachchha book is honored verbatim and not normalized. Recensions of the Nandī opening vary slightly in the count/wording of the Meru-gāthās, so minor variant readings are possible; consult a critical edition where exact wording is decisive.
